planting.note: Transplant container-grown seedlings in spring in very well-drained, dry, pure white sand in full sun; sand pine is adapted to the most nutrient-poor, droughty, xeric white sand soils in Florida and adjacent states where no other pine survives. Do not amend planting soil.

directions: Plant in dry, infertile, pure white to light-colored, acidic sand in full sun; sand pine is a Florida endemic of the ancient marine sand ridges and white sand scrub habitats of the Florida peninsula and coastal panhandle — one of the most ecologically specialized trees in North America. It is the primary tree of Florida scrub, growing in soil so nutrient-poor and drought-prone that it excludes virtually all other tree species. Two populations exist: a serotinous scrub variety (var. clausa) with cones that remain closed until opened by fire — releasing a mass of seeds after fire removes competing vegetation — and an open-cone variety (var. immuricata) along the Gulf Coast. The scrub habitat it creates is among the most biodiverse ecosystems in Florida, harboring endemic species including the Florida scrub jay, Florida mouse, and dozens of rare plant species. An extremely site-specific tree that should only be planted in authentic white-sand scrub conditions.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Montezuma cypress tolerates periodic flooding and performs exceptionally well in the humid, subtropical conditions of Gulf Coast zones 8b–11.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zones 8b–11; Montezuma cypress is the national tree of Mexico, closely related to but more heat-tolerant and less deciduous than bald cypress (T. distichum). In mild climates, it remains semi-evergreen or evergreen year-round — a significant ornamental advantage over the fully deciduous bald cypress. The ancient Tule Tree (El Árbol del Tule) in Oaxaca, Mexico — a Montezuma cypress — is considered the world's largest tree by trunk circumference at over 40 feet in girth, with an age estimated at 1,400–3,000 years. In zone 9–10 Gulf Coast landscapes, Montezuma cypress makes a superb massive shade tree for moist, low-lying areas and streambanks where its greater heat tolerance makes it more vigorous and longer-lived than the native bald cypress. Knees develop in wet conditions, providing habitat for numerous organisms.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants from cuttings in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to deep shade; southern spicebush is a federally threatened species and should only be planted from nursery-propagated stock, never from wild collection.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to deep shade at wetland edges, spring-fed areas, and depression swamps; southern spicebush is a federally threatened native shrub of the southeastern United States, growing naturally in small, disjunct populations in depression swamps, bay swamps, and spring-fed wet areas from Missouri to Florida. A close relative of the common spicebush (L. benzoin) but adapted to wetter conditions and deeper shade, it produces the same intensely aromatic, spicy-fragrant foliage and stems when crushed. Like common spicebush, it is the larval host plant for the spicebush swallowtail butterfly. The beautiful yellow flowers in early spring emerge before the leaves, and the bright red drupes in fall provide excellent wildlife food. Growing this plant in the garden directly supports conservation of a federally threatened species while providing exceptional wildlife value in shaded, wet-site conditions.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in fertile, moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Chinese cinnamon is hardy to about 25°F and can be grown outdoors year-round in zone 9–11. It may defoliate after hard freezes but re-sprouts from the roots.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun in zones 8b–11; Chinese cinnamon is the source of most commercial cinnamon sold in the United States — the cassia bark sold in grocery stores as 'cinnamon' is typically the bark of this or related Cinnamomum species rather than true Ceylon cinnamon (C. verum). The inner bark is harvested by stripping the outer bark from young shoots and allowing it to dry into the familiar quills. Chinese cassia has a stronger, more pungent flavor with higher coumarin content than the mild, sweet Ceylon cinnamon — a trait that limits consumption quantities but makes it the preferred spice for strongly-flavored applications. In zone 9 gardens, this attractive evergreen tree or large shrub provides handsome, dark green, three-veined leaves and an aromatic quality throughout the year. Harvest bark from branches 2–3 years old for optimal flavor concentration.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Korintje cinnamon is slightly more cold-tolerant than Ceylon cinnamon and may survive light frosts to 28°F in protected locations in zone 8b.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist to well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun in zones 9–12; Korintje cinnamon is an Indonesian species from Sumatra that is actually the most commonly sold type of cinnamon in the United States under the generic label 'cinnamon' — accounting for the majority of commercial cinnamon imports. Named for Kota Kerinci in Sumatra, it produces thick, dark-brown bark quills with a strong, mildly sweet flavor intermediate between Ceylon and Chinese cassia cinnamons. The bark is harvested from trees 3–4 years after planting by stripping young branches; coppicing the trees stimulates new shoot production for repeated harvests. Like all commercial cinnamon, the attractive, broad, three-veined leaves are also aromatic when crushed. In zone 9–11, it grows well outdoors as a multi-stemmed shrub or small tree; regular harvesting of branches maintains a bushy, productive form suitable for the home garden.
